comment_text.sn2.9:1.1: This discourse and the next provide the Buddhist community with verses to recite for solace on the terrifying occasion of an eclipse.
comment_text.sn2.9:1.2: Rāhu (“seizer”), is able to swallow the Moon and Sun because he has largest physical body of any creature (AN 4.15:2.1). This also lets him divert the rains (AN 5.197:3.1). This myth is alluded to in Atharvaveda 19.9.10a and Chāndogya Upaniṣad 8.13.1. An eclipse was a day unfit for study (Manu 4.110). The full story of Rāhu, however, is not told until the Purāṇas, so this Sutta is possibly the oldest telling that survives.
comment_text.sn2.9:1.3: Candimā, the “moon” as celestial body or deity, appears frequently in the Vedas (eg. Rig Veda 1.24.10, 1.102.2, etc.). A slightly different form of this name appears in SN 2.11.
comment_text.sn2.9:2.3: The later Purāṇic legend says that Rāhu disguised himself as a brahmin to drink the gods’ nectar of immortality. But he was spied by the Sun and Moon, decapitated by Vishnu, and now his disembodied head traverses the skies as a black planet. He does not forget how the Sun and Moon betrayed him, so, nursing his enmity, he swallows them when he can. He is ever frustrated as they simply pass out through his throat.
comment_text.sn2.9:2.4: Here the sense of “refuge” (saraṇa) is both physical and spiritual.
comment_text.sn2.9:5.1: Rāhu was the son of Vepacitti and, according to Purāṇic legend, Siṁhikā.
comment_text.sn2.9:7.3: Abhigīta first appears with regards to a drop of Soma that has been “chanted over”, “besung”, or “enchanted” and hence imbued with potency (Rig Veda 9.96.23). Throughout Śatapatha Brāhmaṇa 8.5.4 it is used more prosaically: a history of horse sacrifices is listed, each event honored with a specific verse “sung about” it. In Pali, the phrase gāthābhigīta is found only in this passage and when the Buddha refused to eat food “chanted over” by a brahmins (eg. SN 7.8:8.1). In both cases the chanting has a mysterious potency, so I render “enchanted with verse”.

root_text.sn2.9:1.2: Tena kho pana samayena candimā devaputto rāhunā asurindena gahito hoti.
root_text.sn2.9:1.3: Atha kho candimā devaputto bhagavantaṁ anussaramāno tāyaṁ velāyaṁ imaṁ gāthaṁ abhāsi:
root_text.sn2.9:3.1: Atha kho bhagavā candimaṁ devaputtaṁ ārabbha rāhuṁ asurindaṁ gāthāya ajjhabhāsi:
root_text.sn2.9:5.1: Atha kho rāhu asurindo candimaṁ devaputtaṁ muñcitvā taramānarūpo yena vepacitti asurindo tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā saṁviggo lomahaṭṭhajāto ekamantaṁ aṭṭhāsi.
root_text.sn2.9:5.2: Ekamantaṁ ṭhitaṁ kho rāhuṁ asurindaṁ vepacitti asurindo gāthāya ajjhabhāsi:

translation_text.sn2.9:1.2: Now at that time the Moon God had been seized by Rāhu, lord of titans.
translation_text.sn2.9:1.3: Then the Moon God, recollecting the Buddha, at that time spoke this verse:
translation_text.sn2.9:3.1: Then the Buddha addressed Rāhu in verse concerning the Moon God:
translation_text.sn2.9:4.1: “The Moon God has gone for refuge
translation_text.sn2.9:4.2: to the Realized One, the perfected one.
translation_text.sn2.9:4.3: The Buddhas have compassion for the world—
translation_text.sn2.9:5.1: Then Rāhu, having released the Moon, rushed to see Vepacitti, lord of titans and stood to one side, shocked and awestruck.
translation_text.sn2.9:6.3: and came here looking like you’re in shock:
translation_text.sn2.9:6.4: why do you stand there so scared?”
translation_text.sn2.9:7.1: “My head would have exploded in seven pieces,
translation_text.sn2.9:7.2: I would have found no happiness in life,
translation_text.sn2.9:7.3: if, when enchanted by the Buddha’s verse,
translation_text.sn2.9:7.4: I had not released the Moon.”
